title: Soda farls
subtitle: Northern Irish griddle-cooked soda bread, cut into four wedges and cooked flat.
excerpt: Soda farls are Northern Irish soda bread cooked on a griddle instead of baked. The dough comes together in a few minutes with plain flour, bicarbonate of soda, and buttermilk. It is patted into a round, scored into quarters, and cooked flat on a hot pan for about ten minutes. The outside picks up a slight char; the inside stays soft. Serve hot with butter, alongside an Ulster fry or on their own.
sourceNotes: Soda bread in Ireland dates from the mid-nineteenth century, when bicarbonate of soda arrived as a commercial product and transformed bread-making for households without yeast. The farl shape (from the Scots word 'fardel', meaning a fourth part) developed as a practical griddle-cooking method. Florence Irwin's 'Irish Country Recipes' (1937) includes griddle versions close to this one. The Ulster fry, in which soda farls appear alongside bacon and eggs, is documented in Northern Irish food writing from the early twentieth century.

content.text: A soda farl is a round of soda bread dough, scored into four wedges and cooked flat on a griddle rather than baked in a tin. The name comes from 'fardel', an old Scots word for a fourth part. It is Northern Irish breakfast food: fast, simple, and best eaten the moment it comes off the heat.

The dough has four ingredients and comes together in under five minutes. It must go straight onto a hot pan; soda bread dough stales from the moment the bicarbonate of soda meets the buttermilk, so any delay costs you rise.

items.name: Buttermilk
items.prepNote: or whole milk with 1 tbsp lemon juice stirred in, left 10 minutes

content.text: Heat a heavy frying pan or flat griddle over a medium-low heat. Do not grease it.
Mix {{plain-flour}} and {{sea-salt-fine}} in a bowl. Add {{bicarbonate-of-soda}} and stir to distribute.
Make a well in the centre and pour in {{buttermilk}}. Mix quickly with a fork or your hand until the dough just comes together. It should be soft but not sticky. Do not overwork it.
Turn the dough out onto a lightly floured surface and pat it into a round about 20 cm across and 1.5 cm thick. Cut it into four equal wedges with a sharp knife or bench scraper.

Place the four farls on the dry pan. Cook over a medium-low heat for 5 to 6 minutes. The underside should turn pale golden with flecks of darker colour. Flip and cook for another 5 to 6 minutes. The farls are done when they are cooked through to the centre; press the thickest part gently and it should feel firm, not soft and doughy.
Serve immediately. Split each farl through the middle and spread with butter while still hot.

attrs.intro: Farls are simple but the griddle heat is the variable.

items.fix: Lower the heat to medium-low for the second batch. The farls need 10 to 12 minutes total at a gentle heat. Thinner farls cook faster; pat to no more than 1.5 cm.
items.cause: Heat too high; the outside colours before the inside cooks through.
items.symptom: Doughy and undercooked in the centre at 6 minutes per side.

items.fix: Add flour a tablespoon at a time until the dough holds together without sticking to your hands. Weigh both flour and buttermilk for reliable results.
items.cause: Buttermilk too thin, or flour measured by cup rather than weight.

items.fix: Bicarbonate of soda loses potency after a year. Test it by mixing a teaspoon with hot water: it should fizz immediately. Cook the dough within 2 minutes of mixing.
items.cause: Bicarbonate of soda old or batter delayed too long before cooking.

attrs.heading: Troubleshooting
